planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or fall for overwinter harvest, or in early spring in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; winter cress overwinters as a rosette and produces tender leaves through fall, winter, and early spring — edible young and raw, becoming more pungent as it matures. Avoid summer planting.

directions: Direct sow in late summer or fall for winter harvest, or very early spring in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; winter cress is a naturalized European biennial widely established along roadsides, stream banks, and disturbed areas throughout North America, producing dark green, deeply lobed, pungent leaves throughout cold weather — an important wild food and forage vegetable in lean months. The young rosette leaves harvested in fall through early spring are pungent and slightly bitter, excellent as cooked greens (boiling twice mellows the bitterness) or raw in small amounts as a peppery salad green. The early spring flower buds (harvested before they open) are excellent steamed or sautéed like broccoli raab and milder than the leaves. Traditionally gathered as a spring tonic green throughout Europe and Appalachia. Self-sows prolifically to establish permanent patches in moist, disturbed, sunny to partially shaded sites.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or fall for winter harvest in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; upland cress is more tender and milder than Barbarea vulgaris and has been cultivated as a garden salad green in Britain for centuries as a watercress substitute for gardens without streams.

directions: Direct sow in late summer or fall for winter harvest in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; upland cress (early yellowrocket) is a European biennial grown as a cool-season salad green, long cultivated in British kitchen gardens as a dry-land substitute for watercress in gardens without access to streams. The dark green, round-lobed leaves are similar to watercress in appearance and have a pleasant, mild peppery flavor when young and harvested before flowering — less bitter and more refined than winter cress (B. vulgaris). Produces pickable leaves very quickly in cool weather, from fall through early spring. Allowed to bolt, the spring flower buds can be harvested like tender broccoli. An old and underappreciated cool-season salad green that thrives when other salad crops fail in cold weather. Well-suited to fall and winter salad gardening in zones 5–9 where it overwinters reliably.

planting.note: Direct sow in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rapini bolts rapidly in heat — timing is critical. Sow in late summer for fall harvest, or in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost for spring harvest. Does not transplant well; direct sow only. Use successive sowings 2 weeks apart for extended harvest.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rapini (broccoli raab, cime di rapa) is an Italian-heritage cool-season brassica grown for its tender stem tips, leaves, and immature flower bud clusters harvested before the yellow flowers fully open. Harvest the central stem with 6 inches of stem, leaves, and tight bud cluster when buds are still green and closed — this is the prime eating stage. The characteristic pleasantly bitter flavor (due to glucosinolates) intensifies in heat and with age. Rapini is a staple of southern Italian cooking — used in pasta, sautéed with garlic and olive oil, and in sausage dishes throughout Campania, Apulia, and Calabria. Succession sow every 2 weeks from late summer into fall for a continuous harvest through October–November in zone 7+. After the main head is cut, side shoots continue to produce smaller edible buds for several additional weeks.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in late summer for fall harvest in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; napa cabbage bolts rapidly in heat and long days — fall crops are more reliable than spring crops in most of the US. Matures in 55–80 days from direct sowing.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in late summer for fall harvest in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun with consistent moisture; napa cabbage is an Asian cool-season vegetable producing large, upright, cylindrical heads of crinkled, pale green inner leaves with white midribs and a mild, sweet flavor far less sulfurous than European head cabbage. Fall crops are far more reliable than spring crops in most American climates because the long days of spring trigger premature bolting. Time a fall planting so heads mature 2–4 weeks before hard frost. Napa cabbage is essential in Asian cuisines — the primary ingredient in Korean kimchi, used extensively in Chinese stir-fries, hot pot, dumplings, and soups. The heads store well (several months under refrigeration). Popular varieties include 'Minuet', 'Blues', 'Rubicon', and 'One Kilo Slow Bolt'. Thin to 12–18 inches between plants for full head development.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; walking stick kale is biennial, producing a thick, very tall central stem in its second year that can reach 6–10 feet — the dried stems were traditionally harvested in the Channel Islands and Cornwall for use as walking sticks and umbrella handles.

directions: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost, transplant after last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; walking stick kale (Jersey kale) is a traditional biennial brassica of the Channel Islands and Cornwall, where it was grown for the extraordinary height its main stem achieves in the second year — up to 6–10 feet tall — with stems so strong and straight that they were traditionally dried, lacquered, and sold as walking sticks, canes, and umbrella handles, a traditional cottage industry on the Isle of Jersey. The large, dark green, curled leaves produced at the top of the tall stem are edible (though tough) throughout the growing season, and the plant is also fed to livestock. Primarily a curiosity and heritage vegetable today, prized by collectors of unusual brassicas. Requires staking as it grows taller. Harvest leaves from the base upward as needed, leaving the growing tip intact for continued height.
